_schemaNotes: All 7 Oncology modalities plus both Immunology modalities (Anti-Cytokine mAb, JAK Inhibitor) are fully authored (concept, schematicParts, pros, cons, verdict, exampleDrugIds). schematicParts are text labels only — accurate illustrated schematics remain a later polish item, not part of this content pass.

modalities.name: ADC (Antibody-Drug Conjugate)
modalities.concept: An antibody guides a toxic drug directly to cancer cells via a chemical linker — like a guided missile.
pros: Targeted, spares healthy tissue
Very potent payloads
Proven blockbusters
cons: Complex/costly manufacturing
Off-target toxicity
Resistance
modalities.verdict: High value, high execution risk

modalities.name: Targeted mAb
modalities.concept: A lab-made antibody that recognizes one specific protein and blocks its function or flags the cell for immune destruction — from marking cancer cells for attack, to neutralizing a protein that drives high cholesterol, to depleting the immune cells that attack a patient's own joints in rheumatoid arthritis. One of biotech's most versatile and time-tested antibody formats, dating to the late 1990s.
pros: Highly selective for its target, spares healthy tissue
Better tolerated than conventional chemotherapy
Can work through multiple mechanisms at once
cons: Only works if the target protein is present/accessible
Large molecules can't reach some tissue sites (e.g. brain)
Resistance or reduced response can develop over time
modalities.verdict: The foundational modality — proven and safe, but often needs a combination partner for full effect.

modalities.name: Bispecific Antibody
modalities.concept: An antibody engineered to grab two different targets at once — a cancer cell on one arm, a T-cell on the other — physically dragging the immune cell in to attack, with no need to engineer the patient's own cells.
pros: Off-the-shelf — no need to harvest/engineer patient cells like CAR-T
Can achieve CAR-T-like response rates in blood cancers
Fast-growing class: multiple approvals since 2014
cons: Cytokine release syndrome and neurotoxicity risk, similar to CAR-T
Short half-life often requires frequent or continuous infusion
Efficacy in solid tumors still limited
modalities.verdict: A cheaper, more scalable alternative to cell therapy — a fast-growing modality class.

modalities.name: Cell Therapy (CAR-T)
modalities.concept: A patient's own T-cells are extracted, genetically re-engineered to carry a receptor that recognizes a cancer marker, then infused back in as a living drug that can multiply and persist in the body.
pros: Deep, durable remissions in relapsed/refractory blood cancers
Living drug — can persist for years, guarding against relapse
Six approved products since 2017 for hematologic malignancies
cons: Serious toxicities: cytokine release syndrome, neurotoxicity
Very expensive ($300K–$475K per treatment)
Complex, slow, patient-specific manufacturing; limited solid-tumor success so far
modalities.verdict: The most powerful but most operationally demanding modality — reserved for cases nothing else can touch.

modalities.name: Small Molecule (Kinase Inhibitor)
modalities.concept: A small chemical compound taken as a pill that fits into the active site of a mutated enzyme (kinase) driving tumor growth, switching it off — the way a key jams a lock.
pros: Oral dosing — convenient, no infusion needed
High efficacy with relatively mild side effects in the right tumor type
Cheap to manufacture relative to biologics
cons: Resistance mutations commonly emerge over time
Only effective when the tumor is 'addicted' to that specific kinase
Off-target binding can cause unexpected toxicities
modalities.verdict: The workhorse modality — simple to deliver, but resistance is the constant fight.

modalities.name: Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor
modalities.concept: An antibody that blocks the 'brakes' cancer cells use to hide from the immune system (like PD-1/PD-L1 or CTLA-4), releasing the immune system to recognize and attack the tumor.
pros: Durable, sometimes years-long responses in a subset of patients
Works across many tumor types, not just one
Response can persist even after stopping treatment
cons: Only a minority of patients respond
Immune-related adverse events can affect any organ
Response patterns can be unpredictable (pseudo-progression before shrinkage)
modalities.verdict: Transformed oncology by unleashing the immune system — but who responds is still hard to predict.

modalities.name: Radioligand Therapy
modalities.concept: A tumor-seeking molecule is chemically bonded to a radioactive isotope; once it locks onto the target on cancer cells, it delivers a localized dose of radiation that damages the cell from within.
pros: Delivers radiation directly to tumor cells, sparing healthy tissue
Proven survival benefit in prostate cancer and neuroendocrine tumors
Pairs with imaging for a 'see it, treat it' theranostic approach
cons: Requires specialized nuclear medicine infrastructure and handling
Global supply and workforce shortages limit access
Still carries meaningful Grade 3+ adverse event rates
modalities.verdict: A precision-radiation modality with real survival data — bottlenecked by infrastructure, not science.

modalities.name: Anti-Cytokine mAb
modalities.concept: An antibody that neutralizes a single inflammatory messenger molecule (a cytokine like TNF-alpha or IL-17) before it can trigger the immune system to attack the body's own tissue — dialing down chronic inflammation at its source.
pros: Highly effective at inducing remission in autoimmune disease
Decades of real-world safety data (first approved 1998)
Broad utility across rheumatoid arthritis, IBD, psoriasis, and more
cons: Increased risk of infection and reactivated latent infections
Up to half of patients don't respond adequately
High cost (~$20,000+/year) with risk of anti-drug antibodies reducing efficacy over time
modalities.verdict: The founding biologic class for autoimmune disease — effective and well-proven, but not universally effective.

modalities.name: JAK Inhibitor
modalities.concept: A small-molecule pill that blocks JAK enzymes inside immune cells, interrupting the internal relay signal cytokines use to trigger inflammation — shutting down multiple inflammatory pathways at once from inside the cell.
pros: Oral pill — no injections or infusions
Fast symptom relief (weeks, not months)
Modulates multiple cytokine pathways at once, useful when a single-target biologic fails
cons: FDA boxed warning: higher risk of blood clots, heart events, and cancer in some patients
Increased risk of shingles (herpes zoster) infection
Requires ongoing monitoring (bloodwork, cardiovascular risk factors)
modalities.verdict: Fast and convenient, but a more serious safety profile limits use in higher-risk patients.

modalities.name: Integrin Inhibitor
modalities.concept: An antibody that blocks a docking protein (integrin) on immune cells, preventing them from leaving the bloodstream and migrating into inflamed tissue — instead of calming the immune system everywhere, it keeps the attack from ever reaching the gut or brain in the first place.
pros: Gut-selective options (e.g. vedolizumab) avoid whole-body immune suppression
Strong efficacy in inflammatory bowel disease with a favorable long-term safety record
Works by a mechanically different route than cytokine-blocking drugs, useful when those fail
cons: Slower onset of action than some biologics
Non-selective agents (e.g. natalizumab) carry a rare but serious brain infection risk (PML)
Not effective for all autoimmune conditions - mostly gut- and CNS-focused
modalities.verdict: A 'roadblock' strategy instead of a 'ceasefire' - stops immune cells from reaching the target tissue rather than calming them down.

modalities.name: FcRn Inhibitor
modalities.concept: A drug that blocks the cellular 'recycling' receptor (FcRn) that normally keeps antibodies circulating in the blood for weeks — by shutting it off, the body's harmful self-attacking antibodies get flagged for destruction faster, quickly lowering the levels driving the disease.
pros: Rapid reduction of pathogenic antibodies, fast symptom relief
Targets the antibody clearance pathway itself rather than a single cytokine or cell type
Growing list of approved indications beyond its first one (myasthenia gravis)
cons: Also clears some beneficial antibodies, raising infection-risk questions with long-term use
Requires repeat infusions or injections - not a one-time fix
Still a young drug class (first approval 2021), real-world data still accumulating
modalities.verdict: A clever plumbing fix - drains out the harmful antibodies faster instead of stopping the immune system from making them.

modalities.name: Gene Therapy (AAV)
modalities.concept: A modified, harmless virus delivers a correct copy of a faulty gene directly into a patient's cells, letting the body permanently produce the protein it was missing — a one-time treatment aimed at fixing the root cause.
pros: Potentially curative with a single treatment
Restores function in previously untreatable genetic diseases
Long-term expression in non-dividing cells (e.g. neurons, muscle)
cons: Extremely expensive ($850K–$2.1M per treatment)
Immune response to the viral vector can cause liver toxicity or block re-dosing
Limited cargo size — can't deliver very large genes
modalities.verdict: The closest thing to a cure in rare disease — genuinely transformative, but priced and dosed like it.

modalities.name: Enzyme Replacement Therapy
modalities.concept: A lab-made version of a missing enzyme is infused into the bloodstream on a regular schedule, clearing out the toxic buildup that the body can't break down on its own.
pros: Established, well-understood mechanism (first approved 1994)
Meaningfully reduces symptoms and organ damage
Improved life expectancy in several lysosomal storage diseases
cons: Not a cure — requires infusions for life
Can't cross the blood-brain barrier, so neurological symptoms often remain untreated
High ongoing cost ($100,000+/year) with infusion-related allergic reactions
modalities.verdict: Manages disease effectively, but it's a lifelong commitment, not a fix.

modalities.name: Antisense Oligonucleotide (ASO)
modalities.concept: A short synthetic strand of genetic material binds to a patient's faulty RNA and corrects how it's read — either skipping a broken section or blocking production of a harmful protein — without altering the DNA itself.
pros: Reversible — acts on RNA, not permanent DNA changes
Proven to dramatically improve outcomes (e.g. infant survival in spinal muscular atrophy)
Can be redesigned quickly for a new genetic target
cons: Usually requires repeated dosing (e.g. spinal injections every 4 months for Spinraza)
Only treats the specific mutation/exon it's designed for
Delivery to some tissues remains difficult
modalities.verdict: A precise, reversible fix at the RNA level — practical, but not a one-and-done treatment.

modalities.name: RNAi Therapeutics
modalities.concept: A small RNA molecule hijacks the cell's own gene-silencing machinery to destroy the messenger RNA of a disease-causing gene before it can be turned into protein — like deleting the instructions before they're read.
pros: Long-lasting effect from infrequent dosing (as rare as twice a year)
High specificity — sequence-matched to the target gene
Well-tolerated safety profile so far across approved drugs
cons: Delivery is hard — needs a nanoparticle or chemical conjugate to reach cells intact
Still a young drug class (first approval 2018), long-term data limited
Only silences genes — can't add or fix missing function
modalities.verdict: An elegant, durable way to switch off a harmful gene — delivery technology is what unlocked it.

modalities.name: CRISPR Gene Editing
modalities.concept: A patient's own cells are removed, their DNA is precisely edited using CRISPR molecular scissors to correct or disable the disease-causing sequence, then infused back — a permanent edit rather than a temporary patch.
pros: First-ever approved therapy to directly edit human DNA (2023)
Potentially one-time, curative treatment
Strong early real-world results, no serious adverse events reported so far
cons: Very expensive ($2.2M) and requires an arduous stem-cell transplant process
Long-term safety of permanent DNA edits still unknown
Off-target editing risk raises ongoing ethical and safety questions
modalities.verdict: The newest and most powerful tool in the box — enormous promise, but still early days.

modalities.name: GLP-1 Receptor Agonist
modalities.concept: A lab-made version of a natural gut hormone (GLP-1) that boosts insulin release, slows digestion, and signals the brain to reduce appetite — originally a diabetes drug that turned out to be one of the most effective weight-loss treatments ever approved.
pros: Produces double-digit percentage weight loss in clinical trials — unprecedented for a drug, not surgery
Also improves blood sugar control and cardiovascular outcomes, with emerging benefits for liver and kidney disease
Enormous, still-growing commercial demand — among the fastest-selling drug classes in history
cons: GI side effects (nausea, vomiting) are common, especially at higher doses
Requires ongoing, often lifelong use — weight typically returns after stopping
Manufacturing has struggled to keep up with demand, leading to persistent supply shortages
modalities.verdict: The biggest commercial and public-health story in biopharma right now — genuinely transformative, but a chronic therapy, not a cure.

modalities.name: SGLT2 Inhibitor
modalities.concept: An oral pill that blocks a glucose transporter in the kidney, causing excess sugar to be flushed out in urine instead of reabsorbed into the blood — a mechanism discovered almost by accident that turned out to protect the heart and kidneys as much as it lowers blood sugar.
pros: Proven to reduce heart failure hospitalization and cardiovascular death, even in people without diabetes
Slows progression of chronic kidney disease
Oral dosing, with modest weight loss and blood pressure reduction as a bonus
cons: Increased risk of genital and urinary tract infections
Rare but serious risk of euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis (normal blood sugar can mask the danger)
Amputation warning on some labels, though largely tied to one drug in the class rather than the whole class
modalities.verdict: Started as a diabetes pill, became a heart-and-kidney protection drug — one of the best label-expansion stories in modern pharma.

modalities.name: THR-β Agonist
modalities.concept: A daily pill engineered to activate only the liver's thyroid hormone receptors, revving up fat metabolism inside liver cells to clear out the fat and scarring that build up in metabolic liver disease — without triggering the heart and bone side effects of activating thyroid hormone receptors everywhere else in the body.
pros: First-ever FDA-approved treatment for MASH (2024), the fastest-growing liver disease worldwide
Liver-selective design avoids the classic risks of systemic thyroid hormone
Improved liver fat, fibrosis, and cholesterol in pivotal trials
cons: Only approved for moderate-to-advanced fibrosis, not earlier-stage disease
Common GI side effects (diarrhea, nausea)
Still new - long-term outcomes data (e.g. does it prevent cirrhosis/liver failure) still maturing
modalities.verdict: Opened an entirely new drug category for a disease that affects an estimated 1 in 3 adults with obesity - a genuine first-in-class launch.

modalities.name: Insulin (Recombinant/Analog)
modalities.concept: A lab-made version of the hormone the pancreas normally makes to move sugar out of the blood and into cells — the first genetically engineered drug ever approved, and still the backbone of care for anyone whose body can't make enough insulin on its own.
pros: The original proof that genetic engineering could mass-produce a human protein drug (1982)
Life-saving and essentially irreplaceable for type 1 diabetes
Modern analogs (fast-acting, long-acting) offer far more predictable, flexible dosing than older formulations
cons: Requires injections or a pump - no oral form exists at scale
Risk of hypoglycemia if dosing doesn't match food or activity
Pricing controversy in the US despite the molecule being over 40 years old
modalities.verdict: The modality that launched the biotech industry - still essential, still evolving, still occasionally controversial on price.
